steps.title: Identify the clause pattern
steps.content: The sentence first says, Such interpretations are ultimately speculative. That is a complete independent clause. The words after the blank also begin another complete clause: the object's gray coloration may be an incidental effect of radiation, solar wind, or collisions. The punctuation must connect those two complete clauses while using though as a contrast word attached to the first clause.

steps.title: Reject the comma splice
steps.content: Choice B, speculative, though,, leaves two independent clauses joined only by commas. The second complete clause begins after though, so commas alone do not create a strong enough boundary between the two clauses.

steps.title: Reject the dependent opening
steps.content: Choice C, speculative; though, places the semicolon before though. That makes the following words read as a dependent though clause after a semicolon, which does not correctly stand as the second independent clause. The contrast word needs to stay with the first clause before the semicolon.

steps.title: Place the semicolon after though
steps.content: Choice D, speculative, though, makes though introduce the clause that follows it. That turns the second idea into a concessive dependent clause, but the sentence needs that idea to stand as its own independent clause explaining why the interpretations are speculative. Choice A, speculative, though;, attaches though to the first clause with a comma and then uses a semicolon before the next independent clause. The structure is complete clause, though; complete clause, which correctly expresses the contrast. The answer is A.
